title: Vinegret
subtitle: Russian beetroot and vegetable salad dressed with oil and vinegar
excerpt: Diced boiled beetroot, potato, carrot, and pickled cucumber tossed with sauerkraut and dressed with oil. Russia's most enduring salad, vivid magenta, tart, and sturdy enough to make a day ahead.
sourceNotes: Based on the vinegret tradition documented in Elena Molokhovets, 'A Gift to Young Housewives' (1861, public domain in Russia) and Anya von Bremzen and John Welchman, 'Please to the Table' (1990). The salad has been a fixture of the Russian table since at least the nineteenth century.

content.text: Vinegret is one of the great salads of the Russian table. It is simple to make, striking in colour, and much better the day after. The word comes from the French vinaigrette, but the dish is plainly Russian in its mix: boiled root veg, sauerkraut, pickled cucumber, and a dressing of sunflower oil and vinegar. The oil and vinegar tie the earthy beetroot and potato to the sharp fermented veg.

The technique is straightforward. Boil the vegetables separately to preserve their individual textures, beetroot takes longer than potato and carrot and will stain everything magenta if boiled together. Chill, dice, and dress. The sauerkraut is not cooked; it goes in raw, rinsed, and squeezed to reduce its sharpness.

The dicing is where the time goes: everything cut to 8 to 10 mm cubes, as uniform as patience allows. The salad is a series of separate flavours eaten together, and uneven chunks disrupt that balance.

Cook the {{beetroot}} beetroots whole and unpeeled in a large pan of boiling water for 30 to 40 minutes until a skewer slides through easily. Cook the {{potato}} potatoes and {{carrot}} carrots in a separate pan; they take 20 to 25 minutes. Drain all three. When cool enough to handle, peel the beetroots (the skins slip off). Leave everything to cool completely.

Cut the beetroot, potato, and carrot into 8 to 10 mm cubes. Rinse {{sauerkraut}} of sauerkraut under cold water, squeeze out the liquid, and roughly chop if the strands are long. Finely dice {{onion}} onion.

Combine the diced vegetables, sauerkraut, and onion in a large bowl. Whisk {{sunflower-oil}} of sunflower oil with {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper-ground}} of pepper. Pour over the vegetables and toss gently. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour before serving.

items.fix: Ensure all vegetables are cold before combining. The staining happens faster with warm ingredients. The flavour is unaffected; the appearance is purely aesthetic.
items.cause: Beetroot added while vegetables were still warm, or beetroot added before other components were fully cool
items.symptom: Everything has turned the same shade of beetroot purple

items.fix: Rinse and squeeze the sauerkraut thoroughly. Taste the dressing before adding and adjust the vinegar to your preference.
items.cause: Too much vinegar, or sauerkraut not rinsed
items.symptom: Salad tastes too sharp and vinegary

items.fix: Use a waxy potato (Charlotte, Nicola, or similar). Boil until just done, a skewer meeting slight resistance is better than complete softness.
items.cause: Floury potato variety rather than waxy, or overcooked

items.fix: Toss the diced onion with a pinch of salt and a small splash of vinegar and leave for 10 minutes before adding to the salad.
items.cause: Raw onion added without macerating first
items.symptom: Onion taste is too raw and sharp

content.text: Vinegret turns up at the Russian table in almost every setting. It is part of the zakuski spread before a formal dinner. It is an everyday lunch dish. It is also a fixture at the New Year's table, next to Olivier salad and herring under a fur coat. Elena Molokhovets wrote a cookbook that guided Russian middle-class homes through the second half of the nineteenth century. She gives several versions of the boiled-root-and-pickle salad. The version known today (beetroot, potato, carrot, sauerkraut, onion) has been more or less stable since then.
